macro_segments.title: Post-dated documents and alternative dating systems for legal contracts

micro_segments.title: The case of the six-year post-dated contract and Rav Nachman's explanation of alternative dating conventions
Rabbi Yosei's teaching on the six-year Greek rule in Eilam and the chronological basis for dating documents
Rav Acha bar Yaakov's challenge: counting from Seleucid era versus Yetziat Mitzrayim
Discussion of conventional year numbering and the thousands place in dating systems
Rav Nachman's definitive ruling: in the diaspora we count only from the Greek kings

macro_segments.title: Jewish versus non-Jewish systems for counting royal years and their application to dating documents

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Nisan as Rosh Hashanah for kings and holidays, and Tishrei for years and shmita
Rav Chisda's interpretation: Jewish kings count from Nisan, non-Jewish kings from Tishrei
Conclusion that we count documents according to the Seleucid dynasty and Tishrei
Historical shift from Seleucid dating to Briat HaOlam dating around the sixth millennium

macro_segments.title: The meaning of yom ginussia and Roman imperial succession practices

micro_segments.title: Initial definition of yom ginussia as coronation day and the problem of whether Romans appoint king-sons
The prophecy from Obadiah about Rome (Edom) having no hereditary succession of kings
Resolution: yom ginussia means the king's birthday, with reference to both king and prince
Exception clause: the Roman emperor can request his son be appointed, illustrated by Asverus bar Antoninus

macro_segments.title: Antoninus and Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi: political wisdom through parable and secret friendship

micro_segments.title: Antoninus's dilemma: he can request the Senate for one of two things but not both—making his son emperor or granting tax remission to Tiberias
Rabbi Yehuda's parable of the person carrying another on his shoulders with a dove—interpreted as a model for indirect succession
The rationale for speaking in parable: avoiding political intrigue through coded messages per Ecclesiastes
The vegetable exchange between Antoninus and Rabbi: coded messages about his daughter's misconduct
Antoninus secretly sending gold to Rabbi Yehuda disguised as wheat, and Rabbi's response about future benefit
The tunnel between Antoninus's and Rabbi's houses and the killing of slave escorts to maintain secrecy
The incident with Rabbi Chanina bar Chama: Antoninus's refusal to have visitors, and the resurrection of a slave
Antoninus's service to Rabbi as a sign of respect despite royal status, and his hope for Olam Ha-Ba

macro_segments.title: Dispute over Edom's (Rome's) eligibility for Olam Ha-Ba and the teachings of Antoninus and Kti'a bar Shalom

micro_segments.title: Rabbi's response using Obadiah and Yechezkel: those who do not act like Esau can enter Olam Ha-Ba
The Baraita paralleling the interpretation: exclusion only applies to specific kings and officers, particularly Antoninus and Kti'a bar Shalom
The story of Kti'a bar Shalom: the emperor's wound analogy, the defense of the Jewish people, and Kti'a's refusal to obey
Kti'a's argument using the four winds metaphor: the world cannot exist without the Jews
Kti'a's execution and his circumcision as a sign of commitment to Judaism before death
Kti'a's bequest to Rabbi Akiva and the latter's creative interpretation of the inheritance law
The heavenly voice confirming Kti'a bar Shalom's entry to Olam Ha-Ba and Rabbi's teaching on instant versus gradual merit

macro_segments.title: The parallel relationship of Adrachan with Rav and conclusion on friendship across religious boundaries

micro_segments.title: Adrachan serving as waiter to Rav parallel to Antoninus serving Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi
The death of Antoninus and Rav's eulogies: 'the bundle has been untied' marking the loss of close friendship

macro_segments.title: The story of Onkelos: conversion, divine rhetoric, and the power of Jewish faith to transform soldiers

micro_segments.title: Onkelos bar Kolonimus converting to Judaism and the Roman emperor's response with successive troops
Onkelos's first defense: citing verses about the four winds to explain why the world needs the Jews
Onkelos's second argument using the torch-bearer metaphor: God serves the Jewish people
Onkelos's third argument using the mezuza: God protects us from outside while we dwell within
The emperor ceases sending troops after four waves of soldiers all convert to Judaism

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Chanina bar Chama (resurrection of dead)
Conversion of non-Jews through Torah argument
